originalTitle: Senegal parade Afcon trophy despite title controversy
title: Senegal parade Afcon trophy despite title controversy
content: Senegal parade the Africa Cup of Nations trophy before Saturday's friendly match against Peru in Paris - despite being stripped of the title earlier this month.

originalTitle: 'The gravest crime against humanity': What does the UN vote on slavery mean?
title: A Big Talk About Fairness: Learning from History
content:
Hello from Sunny Africa!

Hello, wonderful explorers! It's your friendly reporter, Kiki, here from sunny Africa! Today, we're going to talk about something important that grown-ups are discussing, and it's all about fairness, history, and making the world a better place for everyone. It might sound like a big grown-up topic, but I promise to make it easy for you to understand, like a fun story!

What Was Slavery?

A long, long, long time ago, even before your grandparents were born, there was a very sad time called slavery. During that time, some people were taken away from their homes, especially from parts of Africa. They were forced to work for other people without getting paid and couldn't be free to live their own lives. They couldn't choose where to live or what to do. It was a very, very unfair way to treat people. Everyone agrees that slavery was a terrible thing, a big mistake from the past. It was wrong because everyone deserves to be free and happy. Did you know? Did you know that brave people all over the world worked very hard to stop slavery, and eventually, it was made illegal everywhere?

Why Are We Talking About This Now?

So, why are we talking about this old history now? Well, there's a very big club called the United Nations, or the UN for short. Imagine it like a huge school assembly where countries from all over the world come together to talk about important things. Recently, countries from Africa and some beautiful islands called the Caribbean went to this big UN meeting. They want to talk about the unfairness that happened so long ago. Did you know? Did you know the United Nations has almost 200 countries that are members? They all work together to try and solve big problems and help people around the world!

What Do African and Caribbean Countries Want?

These countries, like Nigeria, Ghana, Jamaica, and Barbados, have people whose ancestors suffered a lot because of slavery. Their great-great-great-grandparents were often the ones who were taken away and forced to work. Now, the leaders of these countries are saying, 'Hey, that was very unfair, and it affected our communities for a very long time.' They want the countries that were helped by slavery to help make things right.

What is "Compensation"?

When we say 'make things right,' we're talking about something called 'compensation.' It's a big word, but it just means trying to fix something that was broken or unfair in the past. Imagine if you accidentally broke your friend's favourite crayon. You would say 'sorry' and maybe help them get a new one, right? That's a bit like compensation. It's not just about money; it's also about saying sorry for the past wrongs and helping to build stronger schools, hospitals, and communities in the places that were hurt by slavery. It's about helping people today because of what happened a long, long time ago. Did you know? Did you know that saying 'sorry' and trying to fix things is a very important part of being a good friend and neighbour?

Is It Easy to Get Compensation?

This is where it gets a little bit tricky, like solving a big puzzle! The countries that were helped by slavery are listening, but it's not a simple 'yes' or 'no' answer. It's hard to decide how to help, who should help, and exactly what kind of help is needed. Many different countries and people have different ideas. So, it's going to take a lot of talking, listening, and working together to find the best way forward. It's a bit like when you and your friends have to decide on a game to play – sometimes it takes a little while to agree!

Why Is This Important for Us?

You might be thinking, 'Kiki, this happened so long ago! Why is it important for me, a Class 2 student, to know?' That's a great question! It's important because learning about history helps us understand the world we live in today. It teaches us about fairness, kindness, and why it's so important that everyone is treated with respect. By remembering the past, we can promise each other that we will always be kind and make sure no one is ever treated unfairly again. Every person on Earth deserves to be free and happy. Did you know? Did you know that Africa is a continent with amazing stories, beautiful cultures, and very strong, kind people?

Working Together for a Brighter Future!

So, even though this is a big and sometimes sad part of history, the important thing is that countries are talking. They are working together, like good friends, to try and make things fairer for everyone. They want to build a future where everyone has a chance to succeed, no matter where their ancestors came from. It's all about learning from the past, saying sorry for wrongs, and working hand-in-hand to make our world a happier, more equal place for you and for generations to come. Keep shining bright, young explorers!
summary: African and Caribbean countries are talking with the United Nations about how to make things fair and help communities today because of the sad history of slavery a long time ago.
whyMatters: It matters because learning from history helps us understand fairness, respect, and how to work together to build a kinder and happier world for everyone.
